title: Nuclearpedia
subtitle: Explore the nuclear encyclopedia to understand basic concepts, technology, and radiation safety.
exploreMore: Let's find out, what is nuclear?

1.title: Surveymeter
1.excerpt: A surveymeter is a measuring instrument used to detect and measure radiation levels in the environment practically.

2.title: Nuclear Reactor
2.excerpt: Learn how nuclear reactors control fission chain reactions to produce heat and electrical energy.

3.title: External Radiation
3.excerpt: Understanding ionizing radiation originating from sources outside the human body and how to mitigate its risks.

4.title: Internal Radiation
4.excerpt: Understand the dangers of radionuclides that enter the body through inhalation, ingestion, or skin absorption.

5.title: Properties of Radiation
5.excerpt: Classification of radiation into ionizing and non-ionizing types and the characteristics that determine their hazard levels.

6.title: Gamma Rays
6.excerpt: High-energy electromagnetic radiation that is highly penetrating, originating from unstable atomic nuclei.

7.title: International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)
7.excerpt: The center of international cooperation in the nuclear field to promote safe, secure, and peaceful use of nuclear technologies.

8.title: Nuclear Applications in Healthcare
8.excerpt: Nuclear techniques play a vital role in the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of various health conditions, particularly cancer.

9.title: Nuclear Applications in Industry
9.excerpt: Nuclear science and technology play a major role in bringing innovation, safety, and efficiency to various industrial processes.

10.title: Nuclear Applications in Agriculture
10.excerpt: Nuclear technology provides competitive solutions to combat hunger, improve environmental sustainability, and ensure global food security.

article1.title: Surveymeter
article1.p1: A surveymeter is a measuring instrument used to detect and measure radiation levels in the environment. These devices are portable, making them practical for field use. A surveymeter can measure radiation levels because it contains a detector. There are 3 commonly used types of detectors: Geiger Muller, Scintillation, and Ionization Chamber.
article1.p2:
- Geiger Muller: The Geiger Muller detector works on the principle of ionization. Radiation entering the tube reacts with the gas inside. This ionization can be channeled to produce electrical pulses. Because Geiger Muller is sensitive to radiation, this detector cannot distinguish between different radiation energies.
- Scintillation: Scintillation detectors produce light when struck by radiation, and this light is measured by a Photomultiplier Tube (PMT).
- Ionization Chamber: Ionization chambers measure the number of ions produced when radiation interacts with the gas inside the chamber.
article1.p3: Surveymeters are important tools for protection against radiation hazards. In operation, surveymeters need to be calibrated regularly. According to Bapeten Regulation No. 1 of 2006, surveymeter calibration is generally performed once a year. Surveymeters that are used frequently should be calibrated more often than those used rarely. Calibration is done to maintain the accuracy level of the surveymeter. There are two general calibration methods: using a standard surveymeter and using a standard source.
article1.source: Source: IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency, IAEA)

article2.title: Nuclear Reactor
article2.p1: The IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency) defines a nuclear reactor as a device used to initiate and control a nuclear chain reaction. Nuclear reactors can be used for various purposes such as electricity generation, scientific research, and radioisotope production for medicine and industry. In general, nuclear reactors function to control nuclear fission reactions that produce energy. This energy can then be used for various purposes, from electricity generation to scientific research and isotope production for medical needs. The National Research and Innovation Agency (BRIN) is developing small-scale nuclear reactor technology called the Small Modular Reactor (SMR). These reactors are designed to produce electrical energy on a small and modular scale, allowing them to be placed in various locations. BRIN is also involved in the development of Generation IV reactors that use gas as a coolant and graphite as a moderator.
article2.p2: The process of generating electricity from a nuclear reactor involves several key stages. Here is a general overview of how electricity is produced from a nuclear reactor:
article2.step1_desc:
o Fission reaction occurs when a heavy atomic nucleus (such as uranium-235 or plutonium-239) absorbs a neutron and then splits into two smaller nuclei, releasing energy in the form of heat as well as new neutrons that can trigger a chain reaction.
o The energy released from the fission reaction is primarily in the form of heat, which is used to heat water.
article2.step2_desc:
o The heat produced from the fission reaction is used to heat water in a closed system, usually in tubes called steam generators.
o This heated water then turns into high-pressure steam.
article2.step3_desc:
o High-pressure steam is directed to a turbine, which is a device with blades that rotate when hit by the steam flow.
o As steam flows through the turbine, the heat energy and pressure from the steam are converted into mechanical energy in the form of turbine rotation.
article2.step4_desc:
o This rotating turbine is connected to an electrical generator.
o The generator converts the mechanical energy from the turbine into electrical energy through the principle of electromagnetic induction, where a moving magnetic field around a conductor generates an electric current.
article2.step5_desc:
o After passing through the turbine, the steam loses most of its energy and is condensed back into water using a condenser.
o The cooled water is then pumped back into the reactor to be heated again, and this cycle repeats continuously.
article2.step6_desc: o The electricity produced by the generator is sent to a transformer to increase the voltage so it can be transmitted through the power grid with high efficiency. o From there, electricity is distributed to homes, businesses, and industries.
article2.source: Source (IAEA, World Nuclear Association, Nuclear Energy Institute)

article3.title: External Radiation
article3.p1: According to the Nuclear Association, external radiation is ionizing radiation that originates from a source external to the human body. This radiation can penetrate the body and cause damage to cells and tissues.
article3.p2: There are three types of external radiation:
article3.types:
• Alpha radiation: Alpha radiation consists of positively charged particles called alpha particles. Alpha particles have low penetrating power and can only penetrate a few centimeters of air or skin. However, if alpha radiation is ingested or inhaled, it can cause serious damage to internal organs.
• Beta radiation: Beta radiation consists of electrons or positrons (anti-electron particles). Beta particles have higher penetrating power than alpha particles and can penetrate several meters of air or several centimeters of tissue.
• Gamma radiation: Gamma radiation is high-energy electromagnetic radiation that has no mass or charge. Gamma radiation has the highest penetrating power of all types of radiation and can penetrate several meters of concrete or several centimeters of lead.
article3.p3: Exposure to external radiation can occur in various situations, including:
article3.situations:
• Working with radioactive materials: People who work with radioactive materials are at risk of external radiation exposure. This includes workers at nuclear power plants, medical facilities, and research laboratories.
• Medical exposure: Patients undergoing medical tests or treatments involving radioactivity are at risk of external radiation exposure. This includes X-rays, CT scans, and radiation therapy.
• Nuclear accidents: Nuclear accidents can cause the release of external radiation into the environment. This can impact people living near the accident site, as well as people exposed to radioactive contamination.
article3.p4:
The effects of external radiation exposure depend on several factors, including:

• Type of radiation: Alpha, beta, and gamma radiation have different effects on the body.
• Amount of exposure: The more radiation a person receives, the greater the risk of experiencing adverse health effects.
• Duration of exposure: Brief radiation exposure is generally less dangerous than long-term exposure.
• Age: Children and infants are more sensitive to the effects of radiation than adults.
• Health: People with weakened immune systems are at higher risk of adverse health effects from radiation.
article3.p5:
The health effects of external radiation exposure can range from mild to severe. Mild effects include fatigue, nausea, and vomiting. Severe effects include cancer, organ damage, and death.

There are several ways to reduce the risk of external radiation exposure, including:

• Limiting exposure time: The less time someone spends near a radiation source, the lower the risk of exposure.
• Increasing distance: The further someone is from a radiation source, the smaller the exposure.
• Protecting oneself: Lead shielding or other materials can be used to protect the body from radiation.
• Monitoring exposure: People who work with radioactive materials must monitor their exposure with dosimeters.

article4.title: Internal Radiation
article4.p1: According to the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), internal radiation is ionizing radiation originating from radioactive sources that have entered the human body. This exposure can occur in various ways, such as:
article4.ways:
• Ingestion: Consuming food or drink contaminated with radionuclides.
• Inhalation: Breathing air contaminated with radioactive dust or gas.
• Skin absorption: Direct skin contact with radioactive materials, such as through wounds or cuts.
article4.p2: Once inside the body, radionuclides can circulate through the bloodstream and lymphatic system, and accumulate in specific organs and tissues. This internal radiation exposure can cause cell and tissue damage, and potentially increase the risk of cancer and other health problems.
article4.f_title: The effects of internal radiation exposure depend on several factors, including:
article4.factors:
• Type of radionuclide: Each type of radionuclide has different properties and effects on the body.
• Amount of radionuclide: The more radionuclides that enter the body, the greater the risk of adverse health effects.
• Duration of exposure: Long-term radionuclide exposure is generally more dangerous than short-term exposure.
• Age: Children and infants are more sensitive to radiation effects than adults.
• Health: People with weakened immune systems are at higher risk of adverse health effects from radiation.
article4.c_title: Control of internal radiation exposure focuses on preventing contamination and removing radionuclides from the body. Here are several control methods:
article4.controls:
• Prevention of contamination: Implementing contamination prevention measures such as using appropriate PPE, maintaining workplace cleanliness, and implementing effective decontamination procedures.
• Reduction of absorption: Reducing radionuclide absorption into the body by controlling exposure pathways (e.g., respiratory, digestive) and using binding agents.
• Enhancement of elimination: Accelerating radionuclide elimination from the body by encouraging excretion through urine, feces, or sweat.
article4.p3: The IAEA provides various guidelines and resources on internal radiation control, including:
article4.resources:
• Basic Safety Standards for Protection against Ionizing Radiation and the Safety of Radiation Sources (https://www.iaea.org/resources/safety-standards)
• Handbook on Radiological Protection (https://www-pub.iaea.org/MTCD/publications/PDF/PRTM-5_web.pdf)
• Model for Estimating Doses to Internal Organs (https://www-pub.iaea.org/MTCD/publications/PDF/eprmedt/Day_1/Day_1-4.pps)

article5.title: Properties of Radiation
article5.st1: Properties of Radiation According to the IAEA
article5.p1: According to the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), radiation is the release of energy in the form of waves or particles from an unstable atomic nucleus. Radiation can be classified into two main types:
article5.types:
• Non-ionizing radiation: This type of radiation does not have enough energy to remove electrons from atoms, so it is not considered harmful to human health. Examples of non-ionizing radiation include radio waves, microwaves, and infrared radiation.
• Ionizing radiation: This type of radiation has enough energy to remove electrons from atoms, creating ions. This can damage cells and DNA, so ionizing radiation can be harmful to human health. Examples of ionizing radiation include alpha particles, beta particles, gamma rays, and X-rays.
article5.st2: Properties of Ionizing Radiation Determining Its Hazard Potential
article5.p2: The properties of ionizing radiation that determine its potential to cause harm to human health include:
article5.factors:
• Type of radiation: Alpha particles, beta particles, gamma rays, and X-rays have different properties and can cause different types of cell damage.
• Energy: The higher the radiation energy, the more damage it can cause.
• Range: Range is the distance radiation can travel through matter. Alpha particles have a short range, while gamma rays can travel very long distances.
• Biological effectiveness: Biological effectiveness is a measure of the relative ability of different types of radiation to damage cells. Alpha particles are more biologically effective than beta particles and gamma rays.

article6.title: Gamma Rays
article6.p1: According to the World Nuclear Association (WNA), gamma radiation is a type of high-energy electromagnetic radiation originating from atomic nuclei undergoing radioactive decay. Gamma radiation has no mass or charge and has the shortest wavelength and highest energy among all electromagnetic waves. This makes gamma radiation highly penetrating, able to penetrate several meters of concrete or several centimeters of lead.
article6.chars:
• High energy: Gamma radiation has the highest energy among all electromagnetic waves, allowing it to penetrate matter more deeply than other types of radiation.
• Short wavelength: Gamma radiation has the shortest wavelength among all electromagnetic waves, allowing it to move in a straight line and not easily hindered by matter.
• No mass or charge: Gamma radiation has no mass or charge, making it unaffected by electric or magnetic fields.
article6.sources:
• Radioactive decay: Gamma radiation is released from atomic nuclei undergoing radioactive decay. Radioactive decay is the process where an unstable atomic nucleus changes into a more stable atomic nucleus by releasing energy in the form of particles or radiation.
• Cosmic rays: Cosmic rays are high-energy particles originating from outside the solar system. When cosmic rays interact with Earth's atmosphere, they can produce gamma radiation.
• Nuclear reactions: Gamma radiation is released in nuclear reactions, such as nuclear fission and fusion. Nuclear fission is the process where a large atomic nucleus is split into two smaller nuclei, releasing energy in the form of heat, light, and radiation. Nuclear fusion is the process where two small atomic nuclei combine to form a larger atomic nucleus, releasing energy in the form of heat, light, and radiation.
• Man-made sources: Gamma radiation is also used in various man-made applications, such as radiotherapy, medical imaging, and sterilization.
article6.st3: Effects of gamma radiation on human health:
article6.effects:
• Acute radiation syndrome: Short-term exposure to high doses of gamma radiation can cause acute radiation syndrome, with symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, fatigue, and hair loss. In severe cases, acute radiation syndrome can be fatal.
• Cell and DNA damage: Gamma radiation can damage cells and DNA, increasing the risk of cancer and other health problems.
• Immune system suppression: Exposure to gamma radiation can weaken the immune system, making individuals more susceptible to infections and diseases.
article6.protection:
• Time: The less time spent exposed to gamma radiation, the lower the risk.
• Distance: The further from a gamma radiation source, the lower the risk.
• Shielding: Protective materials such as lead or concrete can block gamma radiation.
• Personal protective equipment: Personal protective equipment such as gloves, goggles, and respirators can help protect individuals from gamma radiation.

article7.title: International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)
article7.p1: The IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency) is an international intergovernmental forum that serves as the global centerpiece for scientific and technical cooperation in the nuclear field. The agency was established based on the IAEA Statute, which officially came into force on July 29, 1957, after more than 80 countries agreed to its formation in October 1956. This was inspired by US President Dwight D. Eisenhower's "Atoms for Peace" speech before the UN General Assembly in 1953. Headquartered in Vienna, Austria, the IAEA has approximately 180 member states as of 2025, including Indonesia.
article7.p2: Three main pillars support the IAEA mission: Safety and Security, Science and Technology, and Safeguards and Verification. In carrying out its mission, the IAEA develops international nuclear safety standards and verifies through its inspection system that member states comply with their commitments under the Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) to use nuclear materials and facilities only for peaceful purposes.
article7.p3: The IAEA provides peaceful nuclear technology to help developing countries increase access to radiotherapy for cancer patients, generate electricity, improve food production, combat animal diseases, manage water supplies, and protect the environment. In 2005, the IAEA and its then Director General, Mohamed ElBaradei, were awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for their contributions to preventing the use of nuclear energy for military purposes.
article7.p4: As an active member state of the IAEA, Indonesia maintains close cooperation, which is manifested through Physical Inventory Verification (PIV) inspections by the IAEA at several Indonesian nuclear facilities, including the G.A. Siwabessy Multipurpose Reactor and BRIN's TRIGA Reactor, as well as the implementation of the Annual Safeguards Review Meeting with BAPETEN. This cooperation reflects Indonesia's commitment to utilizing nuclear technology safely, responsibly, and entirely for peaceful purposes.
article7.source: Source: IAEA Official Website - iaea.org/about & BAPETEN - bapeten.go.id

article8.title: Nuclear Applications in Healthcare
article8.p1: Nuclear techniques play a crucial role in the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of various health conditions, especially diseases like cancer and cardiovascular diseases. Since the IAEA began its human health program more than 50 years ago, the use of nuclear techniques in medicine has become one of the most widely used peaceful applications of atomic energy worldwide.
article8.p2:
Nuclear medicine involves the administration of radiopharmaceuticals into the body, which are compounds containing small amounts of radioactive material used for diagnosis or treatment. One of the most widely used radioisotopes is Technetium-99m (Tc-99m), applied in bone scans, cardiac imaging, and other organ imaging. Additionally, Fluorine-18 FDG (F-18 FDG) is used as a standard in PET scans to detect cancer cells based on their metabolic activity.

The last two decades have witnessed the development of hybrid imaging that combines anatomical and functional images, such as SPECT/CT, PET/CT, and PET/MR. This technology has produced highly reliable tools for diagnosing various diseases such as cancer, cardiovascular diseases, neurodegenerative disorders, and diabetes, which are leading causes of death worldwide.
article8.p3: Beyond diagnostic imaging, nuclear technology is also widely used in radiotherapy — the treatment of cancer using radiation to destroy tumor cells. Commonly used modalities include external beam radiation therapy (EBRT), brachytherapy (placing radioactive sources directly into or near the tumor), and Targeted Radionuclide Therapy (TRT) such as the use of Lutetium-177 (Lu-177) for prostate cancer and neuroendocrine tumors. This approach allows for more precise treatment by minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissue.
article8.p4: One contribution of nuclear science that often escapes public notice is the sterilization of medical devices using gamma irradiation. This technique is used globally to sterilize syringes, bandages, catheters, and various other single-use medical equipment without using heat or chemicals. This method is more effective, efficient, and safer than conventional sterilization methods, making it the backbone of the modern medical equipment industry.
article8.h5: Other Nuclear Applications in Public Health
article8.p5: Nuclear technology also plays a role in maintaining broader public health, including through improving food safety with irradiation to inhibit the growth of pathogenic bacteria, controlling zoonotic diseases, and managing water supply quality using isotope techniques. These applications make nuclear science relevant not only in hospitals but also at the level of national health systems and food security.
article8.h6: IAEA Programs and the Indonesian Context
article8.p6:
The Rays of Hope Anchor Centres network established by the IAEA has provided training to more than 700 professionals in the use of medical radiation, expanding access to safe and high-quality cancer health services. The IAEA human health program also encourages the application of nuclear medicine through resources such as the Human Health Campus, e-learning modules, and interactive webinars.

In Indonesia, nuclear medicine capacity building is supported by BRIN through research and production of radioisotopes for domestic medical needs. Several large hospitals in Indonesia, such as RSUPN Dr. Cipto Mangunkusumo (RSCM) in Jakarta and RSUP Dr. Sardjito in Yogyakarta, already have nuclear medicine facilities including gamma cameras for SPECT. The development of PET/CT services nationally also continues to be expanded as part of efforts to improve the quality of oncology services in Indonesia.
article8.p7: With an ever-expanding scope of applications — from radiopharmaceutical-based diagnosis, cancer radiotherapy, to medical device sterilization and food safety — nuclear technology has proven itself as an essential pillar in modern healthcare systems. Looking ahead, innovations such as targeted radioisotope therapy and artificial intelligence in nuclear imaging are expected to further expand its benefits for the global community, including Indonesia.
article8.source: Source: IAEA – Human Health: iaea.org/topics/health, IAEA – Nuclear Medicine & Diagnostic Imaging: iaea.org/resources/hhc/nuclear-medicine, PubMed – IAEA Nuclear Medicine Education Programs: pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23561452

article9.title: Nuclear Applications in Industry
article9.p1: Nuclear science and technology play a major role in bringing innovation, safety, and efficiency to various industrial processes. Nuclear and radiation technology applications have contributed for years to improving production efficiency, energy conservation, and environmental protection — from small-scale manufacturing to national-scale production processes.
article9.p2:
In the manufacturing industry, radiation processing technology plays an important role in the production of various everyday products such as electric cables, vehicle tires, plastic films, and surface materials. This process utilizes ionizing radiation — generally from electron beam accelerators or Co-60 sources — to modify polymer properties, resulting in materials that are more heat-resistant, stronger, and more corrosion-resistant than conventional processes.

Online thickness measurement techniques using radioisotope gauges have also become indispensable components in the production of paper, plastic, and steel plates. Radioisotope-based sensors such as Kr-85 or Am-241 allow for continuous measurement of material thickness without physical contact, thereby increasing precision and product consistency in high-speed production lines.
article9.p3:
One of the most critical industrial nuclear applications is industrial radiography — a method of inspecting materials using X-rays, gamma rays, or neutrons to detect cracks, voids, or hidden defects within structures without damaging them. Common radioisotopes used in this technique include Iridium-192 (Ir-192) for thick steel inspection and Selenium-75 (Se-75) for thinner-walled materials.

This technique is widely used to inspect weld joints in oil and gas pipelines, high-pressure storage tanks, as well as building and bridge structural elements. Strict NDT application is a decisive factor for operational safety, particularly in the oil and gas, power generation, and large-scale infrastructure construction sectors.

Digital Industrial Radiography (DIR) technology is increasingly replacing conventional film with electronic detectors that allow real-time inspection, faster image processing, and more efficient data storage. The IAEA actively supports the harmonization of procedures, education, training, and international certification in the field of NDT to ensure uniform safety standards across all member states.
article9.p4:
Radiotracer techniques utilize the tracking properties of radioisotopes to monitor and optimize various industrial processes that are difficult to observe directly. In the oil and gas industry, radiotracers are used to measure fluid flow rates in underground pipes, detect leaks, and evaluate reservoir efficiency. In the mineral processing sector, these techniques help monitor separation efficiency and material distribution within production systems.

Radioisotope-based sealed sources are also widely used in wastewater treatment plants to monitor flow rates, identify blockage points, and evaluate treatment system performance without needing to interrupt ongoing operations.
article9.h5: Nuclear Applications in Hydrology and Environment
article9.p5: Beyond conventional industry, isotope techniques are also utilized in isotope hydrology to track groundwater movement, measure infiltration rates, and assess the sustainability of water resources. These applications are highly relevant for the mining industry, large-scale agriculture, and environmental management, especially in regions vulnerable to water resource pressure like Indonesia.
article9.p6:
In Indonesia, the utilization of nuclear technology in the industrial sector continues to grow under the coordination of BRIN (National Research and Innovation Agency). Co-60 based gamma irradiation facilities managed by BRIN have been used for medical device sterilization, polymer modification, and food preservation research. Meanwhile, the use of industrial radiography-based NDT techniques continues to expand in the infrastructure and energy sectors, in line with the increasing need for stricter inspection standards on national strategic projects.

From manufacturing production lines to infrastructure inspection and natural resource management, nuclear technology has proven its role as a precise, efficient, and reliable industrial instrument. Looking ahead, the development of digital NDT techniques, miniaturization of radioisotope sensors, and integration of radiotracers with data-based monitoring systems are expected to further expand nuclear's contribution in driving safer, more efficient, and sustainable industries.
article9.source: Source: IAEA – Industry (iaea.org/topics/industry), Industrial Radiography (iaea.org/topics/industrial-radiography), Non-Destructive Testing (iaea.org/topics/other-non-destructive-testing), INIS IAEA (inis.iaea.org)

article10.title: Nuclear Applications in Agriculture
article10.p1: Nuclear technology provides competitive and often unique solutions to help combat hunger and malnutrition, improve environmental sustainability, and ensure global food security. The IAEA and the UN Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) have partnered for more than 60 years to help member states use these technologies safely and effectively, supporting more than 200 national and regional technical cooperation projects each year.
article10.p2:
For nearly five decades, plant mutation breeding techniques using ionizing radiation — such as gamma rays or ion beams — have enabled scientists to produce superior new plant varieties. Exposure to radiation on seeds or plant tissues triggers random mutations in genetic material, which are then selected to produce varieties with desired traits such as resistance to pests, diseases, drought, or soil salinity.

To date, more than 3,400 plant varieties produced through mutation techniques have been officially released in more than 70 countries, including rice, wheat, soybeans, bananas, and various other food crops. One of the most successful examples is the Atomita rice variety developed by Indonesia through gamma irradiation, which has higher productivity and better tolerance to marginal land conditions.
article10.h3: Sterile Insect Technique (SIT) for Pest Control
article10.p3:
The Sterile Insect Technique (SIT) utilizes radiation to sterilize male insects in laboratories before they are released in large numbers into the wild. These sterilized male insects will mate with wild females but produce no offspring, so the pest population gradually decreases drastically without the use of chemical pesticides.

The most iconic success of this technique was the eradication of tsetse flies in Zanzibar in 1996 through FAO/IAEA cooperation, which not only improved animal and human health but also restored the local livestock economy. In addition to tsetse flies, SIT is also applied to control fruit flies (Bactrocera spp.) that attack horticultural crops, as well as Aedes aegypti mosquitoes which are vectors for dengue hemorrhagic fever — an endemic disease highly relevant in tropical regions like Indonesia.
article10.p4:
Food irradiation uses ionizing radiation to inhibit the growth of pathogenic microorganisms, slow ripening, and prevent sprouting in post-harvest agricultural products. This technique has proven effective in extending the shelf life of fruits, vegetables, spices, and meat without significantly changing nutritional value or taste.

Unlike thermal sterilization, irradiation leaves no chemical residues and can be applied to products in sealed packaging. More than 60 countries have approved the use of food irradiation for various commodities, making it one of the most promising post-harvest technologies in the global food supply chain.
article10.h5: Soil, Water, and Plant Nutrient Management
article10.p5:
Isotope techniques also play an important role in soil and water management in the agricultural sector. Stable isotopes such as N-15 are used to track the efficiency of nitrogen fertilizers in the soil, helping farmers determine optimal fertilizer doses and timing thereby reducing waste and environmental pollution. Meanwhile, hydrogen and oxygen isotope techniques are utilized to understand the water cycle in the soil and optimize irrigation systems.

This isotope-based approach enables more precise agriculture (precision agriculture) — reducing resource inputs while maintaining or increasing land productivity.
article10.p6: In the field of animal health, nuclear-based techniques such as Radio-immunoassay (RIA) and radioisotope-based ELISA are used to detect livestock diseases quickly and accurately, including foot-and-mouth disease, brucellosis, and avian influenza. Early detection of these zoonotic diseases is crucial to prevent the spread of outbreaks that can have a major impact on the livestock industry and public health.
article10.p7:
The FAO/IAEA Laboratories in Seibersdorf, Austria serve as the global centerpiece for agricultural nuclear technology research and development, covering plant breeding, animal health, insect pest control, and soil and nutrient management. The socio-economic impact of applying these technologies often reaches billions of US dollars each year.

In Indonesia, the utilization of nuclear technology in the agricultural sector has been ongoing since the BATAN era (now integrated into BRIN). In addition to the Atomita rice variety, Indonesia has also developed various other food crop varieties through mutation techniques, and is active in fruit fly control programs using SIT to support national horticultural exports.

From superior variety breeding and pesticide-free pest control to precise soil nutrient management — nuclear technology has proven its role as a strategic partner in achieving sustainable food security. Amid the challenges of climate change and global population pressure, nuclear-based innovations in the agricultural sector will continue to be one of the most relevant keys for the future of world food, including for Indonesia as an agrarian country.
article10.source: Source: IAEA – Food & Agriculture (iaea.org/topics/food-and-agriculture), INIS IAEA (inis.iaea.org)
